agent_output:
Summary of findings (from the platform)
- get_service_health("new-service")
  - status: unhealthy
  - uptime: 97.1%
  - response_time: 49 ms
  - active_connections: 237
  - last_checked: 2026-03-09T12:00:00Z

- get_logs("new-service", severity=error)
  - Recent error messages (examples): 
    - "Memory usage above 90% threshold" (multiple timestamps)
    - "Failed to authenticate request — token expired"
    - "Rate limit exceeded for API endpoint /v1/users"
    - "Disk I/O latency spike detected on node-3"
    - "Certificate renewal completed successfully" (logged as error-level in this stream)

Important constraint
- You said this service was just created with its first deployment and there is no previous version. Because there is no earlier deployment/version to roll back to, a standard rollback is not possible — there is nothing to revert to.

Recommended next steps (pick one and I can perform it)
1) Redeploy a known-good image/version
   - If you have a previous image tag, container digest, or commit ID to restore, create a new deployment using that artifact. This is the functional equivalent of a rollback for a first-time service.
   - I can create that deployment if you provide the image/tag or commit and confirm the rollout.

2) Fix the immediate runtime problems (mitigate while preparing a redeploy)
   - Increase memory/cpu for the service or scale replicas to reduce memory pressure.
   - Investigate and fix token/auth issues and rate limiting (rotate tokens, backoff, increase quota).
   - Check/repair node disk I/O problems or move pods off a noisy node.
   - I can suggest exact resource changes or apply them if you authorize (need desired cpu/memory/replica counts).

3) Tear down and recreate the service
   - If the current deployment is irrecoverable and you prefer a clean slate, delete and create a new deployment with the correct image/config.

4) Attempt an automated rollback now
   - I can call rollback_deployment if you provide a previous deployment_id. Without a prior deployment_id this will fail.

What I will do next if you want me to proceed now
- If you want me to try a rollback anyway, give me the deployment_id to roll back to (or say “rollback latest” and provide the target image/tag), and I will call rollback_deployment.
- If you prefer redeploying a known-good image, provide the image tag or commit and I will deploy it.
- If you want immediate mitigation, tell me which resource adjustments (memory/cpu/replicas) you want and I will apply them.

Which option do you want to take? If redeploying/rolling back, please provide the target image/tag or deployment_id.
